verses.text: But Solomon was building his own house thirteen years, and he finished all his house. He built also the house of the forest of Lebanon; the length thereof was an hundred cubits, and the breadth thereof fifty cubits, and the height thereof thirty cubits, upon four rows of cedar pillars, with cedar beams upon the pillars. And it was covered with cedar above upon the beams, that lay on forty five pillars, fifteen in a row. And there were windows in three rows, and light was against light in three ranks. And all the doors and posts were square, with the windows: and light was against light in three ranks. Guidance: The chapter opens with a quietly damning statistic. Solomon spent seven years on the LORD's temple (1 Kgs 6:38); he spent thirteen years on his own palace complex. The temple is built; the king's residence takes nearly twice as long. The narrator does not explicitly criticize, but the comparison is unmistakable. The most prominent building Solomon constructed was his own. Within the complex, the showpiece was 'the house of the forest of Lebanon' — so called because of the massive cedar pillars (45 of them, in three or four rows depending on the textual reading) that made the interior look like a cedar forest. Dimensions: 100 × 50 × 30 cubits (150 × 75 × 45 feet) — nearly five times the temple's footprint. The function: a great hall used for state ceremonial, military display (1 Kgs 10:17 mentions Solomon stored 300 golden shields here), and royal-court reception. 'Windows in three rows, and light was against light in three ranks' — clerestory windows on opposing walls flooded the interior with cross-light. The architecture is sophisticated and impressive. The chapter is showing us the rise of royal grandeur. The Mosaic-deuteronomic warnings about kings (Deut 17:14-20) included not multiplying horses, not multiplying wives, and not multiplying silver and gold for himself; Solomon is in the process of fulfilling all three. The thirteen-year palace project marks the consolidation of royal magnificence — and the slow inversion of the Israelite king's role from servant of the LORD's people to oriental potentate. The chapter is honest about this. The same king who prayed humbly for a hearing heart now spends nearly twice as long on his own residence as on God's.

And he made a porch of pillars; the length thereof was fifty cubits, and the breadth thereof thirty cubits: and the porch was before them: and the other pillars and the thick beam were before them. Then he made a porch for the throne where he might judge, even the porch of judgment: and it was covered with cedar from one side of the floor to the other. And his house where he dwelt had another court within the porch, which was of the like work. Solomon made also an house for Pharaoh's daughter, whom he had taken to wife, like unto this porch. Guidance: Three more buildings in the palace complex. (1) The porch of pillars — 50 × 30 cubits, a colonnaded hall, perhaps an entrance vestibule between the House of the Forest and the throne hall. (2) The porch of judgment ('porch for the throne where he might judge'). This is the royal hall of justice — the room where Solomon held the throne and adjudicated cases of state importance. The two-harlots case of 1 Kgs 3 would have been heard here in later years. Cedar-paneled floor to ceiling, this room was the visible icon of Solomonic justice — a room designed to make the king visible to litigants, dignified, judicially serious. (3) The king's residence proper — 'his house where he dwelt' — with another court of similar workmanship. (4) A separate house for Pharaoh's daughter, 'whom he had taken to wife.' She was the Egyptian princess of 1 Kgs 3:1; Solomon gives her her own palace within the complex. The chapter is quietly cataloging the foreign-marriage problem. The deuteronomic kingship law had warned: 'Neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away' (Deut 17:17). Solomon's Egyptian wife — the most politically significant of his eventual seven hundred — is given her own residence inside the royal compound. The seed of his later apostasy (1 Kgs 11:1-8) is here in his early reign, built into the palace's floor plan. The chapter is recording the architecture of compromise. Pharaoh's daughter does not get the LORD's temple; she gets her own house. The principle is precise — Solomon is keeping foreign religion at one architectural remove from the LORD's house — but the architecture itself bears the cost. The complex shows us a king who has not yet abandoned the LORD but has accommodated foreign religion just enough to seed the eventual collapse.

All these were of costly stones, according to the measures of hewed stones, sawed with saws, within and without, even from the foundation unto the coping, and so on the outside toward the great court. And the foundation was of costly stones, even great stones, stones of ten cubits, and stones of eight cubits. And above were costly stones, after the measures of hewed stones, and cedars. And the great court round about was with three rows of hewed stones, and a row of cedar beams, both for the inner court of the house of the LORD, and for the porch of the house. Guidance: The masonry quality. All the palace buildings used 'costly stones' (avanim yeqarot — expensive, precious stones) cut to dimension and 'sawed with saws.' The Hebrew specifies the use of saw-marked precision-cutting on every face — even the back faces of stones that would never be seen. The foundation stones were 8-10 cubits long (12-15 feet) — enormous monolithic blocks, the kind only the largest royal projects could afford. The construction technique mirrored the temple: three courses of cut stone topped by one course of cedar beams, the standard Phoenician earthquake-resistant pattern, used for both the temple's inner court and now the palace courts. The chapter is noting the equivalence of construction quality between the temple and the palace. Both got the costly stones; both got the Phoenician three-stone-plus-cedar coursing. The same level of architectural investment, the same workforce, the same craftsmanship. The neutrality of the description hides the implicit critique. The Mosaic standard for kingly building was modest; Solomon has matched the temple's quality with his palace's. The temple was the building dedicated to the LORD; the palace is the building dedicated to Solomon. They are equally lavish. The chapter is showing us a king whose personal estate increasingly mirrors what should have been God's exclusive due of magnificence.

And king Solomon sent and fetched Hiram out of Tyre. He was a widow's son of the tribe of Naphtali, and his father was a man of Tyre, a worker in brass: and he was filled with wisdom, and understanding, and cunning to work all works in brass. And he came to king Solomon, and wrought all his work. Guidance: A second Hiram enters the narrative — not the king of Tyre (who appeared in chapter 5) but a master craftsman who shares the king's name. The narrator notes his mixed parentage: his mother was an Israelite widow from the tribe of Naphtali; his father was a Tyrian bronze-worker. (2 Chr 2:14 specifies his mother was from the tribe of Dan; the two passages may indicate she was a Danite widow who lived in Naphtali, or there is a textual variant.) The bronze worker himself was therefore half-Israelite, half-Phoenician — a man who could move easily between the two worlds. The chapter describes him with three words: 'filled with wisdom, and understanding, and cunning' — chochmah, tevunah, da'at. These are the same three words used in Exod 31:3 for Bezalel, the master craftsman of the tabernacle. The chapter is making the connection explicit: as the LORD raised up Bezalel to craft the tabernacle, He has raised up Hiram of Tyre to craft the temple furnishings. The Spirit-of-God-given craftsman is one of the consistent biblical patterns — God's tabernacle and temple were not built by amateurs but by Spirit-equipped specialists. The chapter is teaching us that skilled artisanship is a divine gift, and the LORD's projects make full use of such gifts. Hiram of Naphtali-Tyre is, in his way, as much a servant of the LORD's purposes as the priest or the prophet. His hands shaped the bronze that worshipers would gaze at every day they entered the temple court.

For he cast two pillars of brass, of eighteen cubits high apiece: and a line of twelve cubits did compass either of them about. And he made two chapiters of molten brass, to set upon the tops of the pillars: the height of the one chapiter was five cubits, and the height of the other chapiter was five cubits: And nets of checker work, and wreaths of chain work, for the chapiters which were upon the top of the pillars; seven for the one chapiter, and seven for the other chapiter. And he made the pillars, and two rows round about upon the one network, to cover the chapiters that were upon the top, with pomegranates: and so did he for the other chapiter. And the chapiters that were upon the top of the pillars were of lily work in the porch, four cubits. And the chapiters upon the two pillars had pomegranates also above, over against the belly which was by the network: and the pomegranates were two hundred in rows round about upon the other chapiter. And he set up the pillars in the porch of the temple: and he set up the right pillar, and called the name thereof Jachin: and he set up the left pillar, and called the name thereof Boaz. And upon the top of the pillars was lily work: so was the work of the pillars finished. Guidance: The two great pillars — Jachin and Boaz — perhaps the most famous architectural elements of Solomon's temple. The pillars stood in the porch (ulam) flanking the entrance, not supporting it structurally; they were freestanding monumental columns of the type common at Phoenician and Cypriot temples. Their dimensions: 18 cubits high (27 feet), 12 cubits circumference (so about 4 cubits / 6 feet diameter — massive). On top, capitals (chapiters) 5 cubits high — another 7.5 feet — bringing each pillar's total height to about 35 feet. The decoration: each capital had networks of checker work and chain work; two rows of 100 pomegranates each (so 200 per capital, 400 total) hanging from chains; lily-work at the top. Pomegranates and lilies were standard ancient near-east motifs of fruitfulness and beauty. The names. The right pillar was called 'Jachin' (yakhin — 'He will establish'). The left pillar was called 'Boaz' (boaz — possibly 'in him is strength' or simply 'strength is in him'). Some scholars read the two names together as the opening words of a now-lost dedicatory inscription: 'He will establish in strength.' The names declare what the LORD does for His house — establishes and strengthens. The pillars stood as a constant visible reminder to every worshiper entering the porch: this house stands because the LORD establishes it; its strength is His. Pilgrims passing between them moved from the world outside, where strength and stability are uncertain, into the LORD's presence, where stability is His gift. The chapter is teaching us that even the architectural features of holy places can speak: the pillars proclaimed a theology every time someone walked between them. The detail of '200 pomegranates in rows' — quantified, deliberate — emphasizes the bountiful fruitfulness associated with the LORD's establishing strength. The fruit was always visible at the entry to His house.

On top, capitals (chapiters) 5 cubits high — another 7.5 feet — bringing each pillar's total height to about 35 feet. The decoration: each capital had networks of checker work and chain work; two rows of 100 pomegranates each (so 200 per capital, 400 total) hanging from chains; lily-work at the top. Pomegranates and lilies were standard ancient near-east motifs of fruitfulness and beauty. The names. The right pillar was called 'Jachin' (yakhin — 'He will establish'). The left pillar was called 'Boaz' (boaz — possibly 'in him is strength' or simply 'strength is in him'). Some scholars read the two names together as the opening words of a now-lost dedicatory inscription: 'He will establish in strength.' The names declare what the LORD does for His house — establishes and strengthens. The pillars stood as a constant visible reminder to every worshiper entering the porch: this house stands because the LORD establishes it; its strength is His. Pilgrims passing between them moved from the world outside, where strength and stability are uncertain, into the LORD's presence, where stability is His gift. The chapter is teaching us that even the architectural features of holy places can speak: the pillars proclaimed a theology every time someone walked between them. The detail of '200 pomegranates in rows' — quantified, deliberate — emphasizes the bountiful fruitfulness associated with the LORD's establishing strength. The fruit was always visible at the entry to His house.

For he cast two pillars of brass, of eighteen cubits high apiece: and a line of twelve cubits did compass either of them about. And he made two chapiters of molten brass, to set upon the tops of the pillars: the height of the one chapiter was five cubits, and the height of the other chapiter was five cubits: And nets of checker work, and wreaths of chain work, for the chapiters which were upon the top of the pillars; seven for the one chapiter, and seven for the other chapiter. And he made the pillars, and two rows round about upon the one network, to cover the chapiters that were upon the top, with pomegranates: and so did he for the other chapiter. And the chapiters that were upon the top of the pillars were of lily work in the porch, four cubits. And the chapiters upon the two pillars had pomegranates also above, over against the belly which was by the network: and the pomegranates were two hundred in rows round about upon the other chapiter. And he set up the pillars in the porch of the temple: and he set up the right pillar, and called the name thereof Jachin: and he set up the left pillar, and called the name thereof Boaz. And upon the top of the pillars was lily work: so was the work of the pillars finished. And he made a molten sea, ten cubits from the one brim to the other: it was round all about, and his height was five cubits: and a line of thirty cubits did compass it round about. And under the brim of it round about there were knops compassing it, ten in a cubit, compassing the sea round about: the knops were cast in two rows, when it was cast. It stood upon twelve oxen, three looking toward the north, and three looking toward the west, and three looking toward the south, and three looking toward the east: and the sea was set above upon them, and all their hinder parts were inward. And it was an hand breadth thick, and the brim thereof was wrought like the brim of a cup, with flowers of lilies: it contained two thousand baths. Guidance: The molten sea — one of the most striking objects in the temple complex. A massive cast-bronze basin, 10 cubits in diameter (15 feet across), 5 cubits high (7.5 feet), 30 cubits in circumference (so the ratio is approximately π, the standard ancient-Near-Eastern engineering approximation). It held 2,000 baths (about 12,000 gallons / 45,000 liters) — a swimming-pool-sized basin of water. The bronze was a handbreadth thick (about 3 inches). The basin rested on twelve life-sized bronze oxen, three facing each of the four cardinal directions, their hindquarters meeting under the basin in the center. The brim was decorated with lily-work and a band of gourd-like knops in two rows. Function: priestly washing. 2 Chr 4:6 specifies that the sea was 'for the priests to wash in.' Before performing sacrifices, the priests would draw water from the molten sea to wash hands and feet. The huge volume ensured a fresh supply for the constant ritual washing required in temple service. Symbolism: 'Sea' (yam) in Hebrew thought represented the chaotic waters of creation that the LORD tamed at the beginning (Gen 1:6-10), reframed by Moses at the Red Sea (Exod 14), and that prophets would see eternally defeated in the messianic age (Isa 27:1). The temple had its own miniature sea — the chaos waters now contained, organized, and put in the service of holy worship. The twelve oxen bearing the sea — three to each direction — represent the twelve tribes (the entire covenant people) holding up the LORD's contained chaos. The picture is theological: the LORD's people surround and uphold the cleansing waters that purify the priesthood. The chapter is showing us a piece of architectural symbolism that prefigures, for Christian readers, the cleansing waters of baptism: water provided for purification, made possible by the labor of the LORD's people, available to those who minister to God. The molten sea was visible to all who entered the inner court — a public reminder that the LORD's holy work requires constant cleansing.

And he made ten bases of brass; four cubits was the length of one base, and four cubits the breadth thereof, and three cubits the height of it. And the work of the bases was on this manner: they had borders, and the borders were between the ledges: And on the borders that were between the ledges were lions, oxen, and cherubims: and upon the ledges there was a base above: and beneath the lions and oxen were certain additions made of thin work. And every base had four brasen wheels, and plates of brass: and the four corners thereof had undersetters: under the laver were undersetters molten, at the side of every addition. And the mouth of it within the chapiter and above was a cubit: but the mouth thereof was round after the work of the base, a cubit and an half: and also upon the mouth of it were gravings with their borders, foursquare, not round. And under the borders were four wheels; and the axletrees of the wheels were joined to the base: and the height of a wheel was a cubit and half a cubit. And the work of the wheels was like the work of a chariot wheel: their axletrees, and their naves, and their felloes, and their spokes, were all molten. And there were four undersetters to the four corners of one base: and the undersetters were of the very base itself. And in the top of the base was there a round compass of half a cubit high: and on the top of the base the ledges thereof and the borders thereof were of the same. For on the plates of the ledges thereof, and on the borders thereof, he graved cherubims, lions, and palm trees, according to the proportion of every one, and additions round about. After this manner he made the ten bases: all of them had one casting, one measure, and one size. Guidance: The ten wheeled bronze stands — possibly the most technically sophisticated objects ever made in Old Testament Israel. Ten identical four-wheeled bronze chariot-like platforms, each 4 × 4 × 3 cubits (6 × 6 × 4.5 feet). Each platform supported a bronze laver (basin) on top. The function: mobile water stations for washing sacrificial animals. Where the molten sea served the priests, the ten lavers served the offerings — meat being prepared for the altar. Construction details: each base was a complex cast-bronze panel-and-frame assembly with bordered panels carved with cherubim, lions, oxen, and palm trees (the same Edenic-imperial iconography as the temple walls). Each had four brass wheels with axles, naves (hubs), spokes, and felloes (rims) — 'after the work of a chariot wheel.' The bases were not stationary; they were mobile. Lavers on wheels meant the cleansing water could be brought to the carcass, instead of carcasses being dragged to the water. The engineering was sophisticated: cast-bronze chariot-style wheels strong enough to bear a bronze laver containing forty baths (about 240 gallons) of water — that's about a ton of water plus the basin. The whole assembly moved on bronze wheels with bronze axles. The repetition of 'one casting, one measure, one size' for all ten stands emphasizes the precision of the work — identical castings of considerable complexity. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's worship requires mobile cleansing-stations: water available where it is needed. The cherubim-lion-ox-palm engravings on each base wrapped the practical machinery in the iconography of the temple. Even the most utilitarian piece of cultic equipment was a meditative object — washing meat for sacrifice was carried out within view of cherubim and palms recalling Eden. The chapter is teaching us that there are no merely utilitarian objects in God's house; everything ministers to worship in its way, and even the most practical articles are dignified with sacred decoration.

And he made ten bases of brass; four cubits was the length of one base, and four cubits the breadth thereof, and three cubits the height of it. And the work of the bases was on this manner: they had borders, and the borders were between the ledges: And on the borders that were between the ledges were lions, oxen, and cherubims: and upon the ledges there was a base above: and beneath the lions and oxen were certain additions made of thin work. And every base had four brasen wheels, and plates of brass: and the four corners thereof had undersetters: under the laver were undersetters molten, at the side of every addition. And the mouth of it within the chapiter and above was a cubit: but the mouth thereof was round after the work of the base, a cubit and an half: and also upon the mouth of it were gravings with their borders, foursquare, not round. And under the borders were four wheels; and the axletrees of the wheels were joined to the base: and the height of a wheel was a cubit and half a cubit. And the work of the wheels was like the work of a chariot wheel: their axletrees, and their naves, and their felloes, and their spokes, were all molten. And there were four undersetters to the four corners of one base: and the undersetters were of the very base itself. And in the top of the base was there a round compass of half a cubit high: and on the top of the base the ledges thereof and the borders thereof were of the same. For on the plates of the ledges thereof, and on the borders thereof, he graved cherubims, lions, and palm trees, according to the proportion of every one, and additions round about. After this manner he made the ten bases: all of them had one casting, one measure, and one size. Construction details: each base was a complex cast-bronze panel-and-frame assembly with bordered panels carved with cherubim, lions, oxen, and palm trees (the same Edenic-imperial iconography as the temple walls). Each had four brass wheels with axles, naves (hubs), spokes, and felloes (rims) — 'after the work of a chariot wheel.' The bases were not stationary; they were mobile. Lavers on wheels meant the cleansing water could be brought to the carcass, instead of carcasses being dragged to the water. The engineering was sophisticated: cast-bronze chariot-style wheels strong enough to bear a bronze laver containing forty baths (about 240 gallons) of water — that's about a ton of water plus the basin. The whole assembly moved on bronze wheels with bronze axles. The repetition of 'one casting, one measure, one size' for all ten stands emphasizes the precision of the work — identical castings of considerable complexity. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's worship requires mobile cleansing-stations: water available where it is needed. The cherubim-lion-ox-palm engravings on each base wrapped the practical machinery in the iconography of the temple. Even the most utilitarian piece of cultic equipment was a meditative object — washing meat for sacrifice was carried out within view of cherubim and palms recalling Eden. The chapter is teaching us that there are no merely utilitarian objects in God's house; everything ministers to worship in its way, and even the most practical articles are dignified with sacred decoration.

And he made ten bases of brass; four cubits was the length of one base, and four cubits the breadth thereof, and three cubits the height of it. And the work of the bases was on this manner: they had borders, and the borders were between the ledges: And on the borders that were between the ledges were lions, oxen, and cherubims: and upon the ledges there was a base above: and beneath the lions and oxen were certain additions made of thin work. And every base had four brasen wheels, and plates of brass: and the four corners thereof had undersetters: under the laver were undersetters molten, at the side of every addition. And the mouth of it within the chapiter and above was a cubit: but the mouth thereof was round after the work of the base, a cubit and an half: and also upon the mouth of it were gravings with their borders, foursquare, not round. And under the borders were four wheels; and the axletrees of the wheels were joined to the base: and the height of a wheel was a cubit and half a cubit. And the work of the wheels was like the work of a chariot wheel: their axletrees, and their naves, and their felloes, and their spokes, were all molten. And there were four undersetters to the four corners of one base: and the undersetters were of the very base itself. And in the top of the base was there a round compass of half a cubit high: and on the top of the base the ledges thereof and the borders thereof were of the same. For on the plates of the ledges thereof, and on the borders thereof, he graved cherubims, lions, and palm trees, according to the proportion of every one, and additions round about. After this manner he made the ten bases: all of them had one casting, one measure, and one size. Then made he ten lavers of brass: one laver contained forty baths: and every laver was four cubits: and upon every one of the ten bases one laver. And he put five bases on the right side of the house, and five on the left side of the house: and he set the sea on the right side of the house eastward over against the south. Guidance: The lavers and their placement. Each of the ten bronze basins on the wheeled stands held 40 baths (about 240 gallons / 900 liters) — large enough for serious washing operations. They were 4 cubits across (6 feet diameter). The placement: five on the south side of the temple, five on the north side, with the molten sea positioned at the southeast corner. The chapter is showing us the working geography of the temple courts. A priest beginning sacrificial work could go to either the southern row or the northern row of lavers, depending on which altar position he was serving. The molten sea, at the southeast — closest to the porch entrance and the morning sun — served the priests for their own washing before they could begin any sacrificial work. The numerology: ten lavers + one sea = eleven washing-stations in total. Worship requires this density of cleansing equipment because Israelite worship was constantly engaged with blood, viscera, and ritual impurity. Every offering required washing both of the animal and the priest. The chapter is teaching us a sober principle: holiness is not avoidance of mess; it is mess engaged through the LORD's provided cleansing. The temple was a place of considerable physical labor — animals slaughtered, blood drained, organs washed, fires tended, meat cooked. Eleven washing stations made the labor possible. Holiness happened in the midst of physical reality, with the LORD providing what made the reality bearable to His presence.

And Hiram made the lavers, and the shovels, and the basons. So Hiram made an end of doing all the work that he made king Solomon for the house of the LORD: The two pillars, and the two bowls of the chapiters that were on the top of the two pillars; and the two networks, to cover the two bowls of the chapiters which were upon the top of the pillars; And four hundred pomegranates for the two networks, even two rows of pomegranates for one network, to cover the two bowls of the chapiters that were upon the pillars; And the ten bases, and ten lavers on the bases; And one sea, and twelve oxen under the sea; And the pots, and the shovels, and the basons: and all these vessels, which Hiram made to king Solomon for the house of the LORD, were of bright brass. In the plain of Jordan did the king cast them, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zarthan. And Solomon left all the vessels unweighed, because they were exceeding many: neither was the weight of the brass found out. Guidance: The summary inventory and the casting location. (1) The list, recited as a closing inventory: two pillars, two capitals, two networks, 400 pomegranates, ten stands, ten lavers, one sea, twelve oxen, plus the secondary equipment (pots for ashes, shovels, basons). All in 'bright brass' (nechoshet memorat — polished bronze; the bronze was scoured/burnished to mirror-finish). (2) The casting location: 'in the plain of Jordan… in the clay ground between Succoth and Zarthan.' The Jordan plain, about thirty miles northeast of Jerusalem, had the right clay deposits for molding such large objects. Bronze casting requires firm fine-grained earth that can be packed into mold-cavities and withstand the pour without cracking. The Succoth-Zarthan clay was famous for this property; ancient bronze workshops have been excavated in that area confirming the textual detail. The size of the objects (the molten sea alone weighed perhaps 25 tons of cast bronze) meant they could not be made anywhere with smaller-scale facilities. They were cast in the field and transported up to Jerusalem. (3) The closing line: 'Solomon left all the vessels unweighed, because they were exceeding many: neither was the weight of the brass found out.' Hebrew lo nechqar mishqal ha-nechoshet — 'the weight of the bronze was not investigated.' The quantity was simply too great to count. The chapter is showing us the scale of the project. The bronze for the temple was not a few tons; it was so much that even careful royal accounting did not bother to tally it. The narrator records the un-tallying as itself testimony to abundance. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's house received gifts at a scale beyond counting — a foreshadowing of the kind of devotion that ultimately characterizes those who give themselves wholly to God's purposes. The widow's two mites (Mark 12:42) and Solomon's unweighed bronze both bear witness to the same principle, from opposite directions: when devotion is real, the giver no longer asks 'how much' — only 'is the work done.'
